---
title: "Testing the robustness of a published claim: Teney (2016)"
author: "Pair: Henry Rong"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: false
    df-print: kable
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Packages used across the report. They are all pre-installed on the workshop
# machines and all available in the browser track too. If any are missing on
# your own laptop, run R/check_setup.R – it tells you which.
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)
library(fixest)
library(plm)
library(modelsummary)

# A calm default look for every figure.
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))
options(scipen = 6, digits = 4)
```

## The claim, and what we are doing to it

Teney (2016) studied how citizens frame the European Union. One claim from that paper is our target:

> "… poor economic performances … decrease positive dimensions of EU framing" (Teney 2016: 619)

Operationalised the way the Multi100 project constrained it for its five analysts: does a country's **unemployment rate** (poor economic performance) lower the **cosmopolitan** dimension of EU framing (peace, democracy, free movement, cultural diversity, a stronger say in the world)? The published anchor result was a coefficient of −0.0034, t = −4.03.

You will hold the data, the tools, and the workflow the project used, and ask: how robust is that number to *justifiable* alternative choices? Work through the tasks below in order. Each is a small, committable step.

::: callout-tip
## How the tasks work

Every task is a callout box with instructions, a **TODO** you complete, and code chunks that **already run** – so the report renders from the first preview. You edit the prose blanks and, in Tasks D2 and F, the code. Keep committing as you go.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-note title="Task A – Concepts"}
**Before touching data, fix the vocabulary.** In the four scenarios below, three are doing one of our three R's, while one is doing something else entirely.

**TODO: Read the four scenarios and fill in the gaps**

***Scenario 1:*** A reviewer reruns the authors' Stata `.do` file on the authors' data and gets a *different* coefficient from the one printed in the paper. This is an example of **reproducibility**, even though what they *tried* to do failed.
***Scenario 2:*** The analyst uses the authors' submitted replication dataset, but they re-implement the model in R instead of Stata and centre the predictor. Is this a case of **robustness**?
***Scenario 3: *** A preregistered team tests the identical hypothesis on a *separate, much larger* dataset chosen to be theoretically equivalent, and gets results that contradict the original. Their conclusion is that the original analysis has failed to **replicate**.
***Scenario 4:*** Instead of the survey data used by the original authors, another researcher tests the same underlying idea on social-media texts using a machine-learning classifier method that the original never used. To their surprise, the authors' original findings prove **generalisability** and the researcher concludes that they provide a reliable middle-range theory of the phenomenon they investigate.

When you have filled these in, commit: `git commit -am "Task A: define the three Rs"`.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-note title="Task B – Grab the data from the web"}
`R/get_data.R` fetches the published `rep_data.csv` from the Open Science Framework – the analyst's fork (OSF node `6zqct`) – with fallbacks. The file already ships in `data/`, so this is an OSF-access *exercise*: running it proves you can pull a real published artefact. If you are offline or the url-fetch gets blocked, it simply uses the local copy.

**TODO:** run the chunks, then read the small-multiples figure. Which countries trend down over 2004–2013? Note one in a comment.

```{r}
#| label: get-data
# Fetch (or confirm) data/rep_data.csv. Prints which route it used.
source("R/get_data.R")
```

```{r}
#| label: read-data
# The published country-year panel: 27 countries x 10 years = 270 rows.
rep <- read_csv("data/rep_data.csv")
glimpse(rep)
```

```{r}
#| label: count-data
# How many countries, how many years, how many country-year cells?
rep |>
  summarise(
    countries = n_distinct(cntry),
    years     = n_distinct(year),
    rows      = n()
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-cosmo-trend
#| fig-cap: "Mean cosmopolitan EU framing over time, by country – an echo of Teney's Figure 1."
ggplot(rep, aes(x = year, y = mcosmopolitan)) +
  geom_line() +
  facet_wrap(~ cntry) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2004, 2008, 2013)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean cosmopolitan framing (0–1)")
```
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-note title="Task C – Estimand and DAG"}
Before estimating, say *precisely* what you are estimating. An estimand has two parts: a **unit-specific quantity** and a **target population** (Lundberg et al. 2021). State it outside any model.

**TODO – complete the estimand sentence:**

> Unit-specific quantity: the change in \[\_\_\_\_\] for a one-point difference in \[\_\_\_\_\]. Target population: \[\_\_\_\_\].

*(A defensible filling: the change in mean cosmopolitan framing within a country-year, for a one-percentage-point difference in that country's unemployment rate; across EU member states, 2004–2013.)*

Now the identification assumptions. `R/dag_starter.R` builds a half-drawn DAG: the causal path and two confounder pairs are in; the more arguable arrows are listed as ready-to-paste lines. Open the file, uncomment any edges you believe, re-source it, and re-run `adjustmentSets()`.

```{r}
#| label: dag
#| fig-cap: "The starter DAG: unemployment → framing, with country and year as confounders."
#| fig-height: 5
source("R/dag_starter.R")
plot_dag(dag)
```

```{r}
#| label: adjustment-set
# What must we adjust for to identify the unemployment -> framing effect?
dagitty::adjustmentSets(dag, exposure = "unemployment", outcome = "framing")
```

The starter graph returns `{ country, year }`. That is the whole point of the two-way fixed effects you will fit next: country fixed effects adjust for `country`, year fixed effects adjust for `year`.

**TODO – answer two questions in a sentence each:**

1.  What do **country** and **year** fixed effects adjust for, in plain terms? *(country-constant traits, and shocks common to all countries in a year)*
2.  What can they **not** fix? *(a time-varying, country-level confounder – e.g. GDP growth moving both unemployment and framing within a country over time)*

Commit: `git commit -am "Task C: estimand + DAG + adjustment set"`.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

:::: {.callout-note title="Task D1 – Reproduce the constrained result"}
Fit the Multi100-constrained model: cosmopolitan framing on (mean-centred) unemployment, with country and year fixed effects. We fit it **two ways** – with `plm` (what the published analyst used) and with `fixest::feols` (faster, and what the live demo uses). They should agree; that they do is itself a small robustness lesson.

```{r}
#| label: fit-rep
# Mean-centre unemployment, as the published analysis did.
rep <- rep |> mutate(unemp_c = unemp - mean(unemp, na.rm = TRUE))

# plm: the published estimator.
m_plm <- plm(
  mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c,
  data   = rep,
  index  = c("cntry", "year"),
  effect = "twoways",
  model  = "within"
)

# fixest: the same model, fixed effects after the | .
m_feols <- feols(mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = rep)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-rep
#| tbl-cap: "The constrained model on the published rep_data.csv, two estimators."
modelsummary(
  list("plm (two-way FE)" = m_plm, "fixest (two-way FE)" = m_feols),
  estimate  = "{estimate}",
  statistic = "t = {statistic}",
  gof_map   = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
  coef_rename = c("unemp_c" = "Unemployment (centred)")
)
```

You just recovered **t = −3.853** on `rep_data.csv`.

::: {.callout-important title="Why the discrepancy?"}
The result recorded for this analyst (C6HJR) in the Multi100 dataset is **t = −3.804**, not −3.853. Why is that?

The original data-prep script read the EB 70.1 file (ZA4819) twice by mistake: once for 2008 (correct) and again in place of EB 72.4 (ZA4994) for 2009 (incorrect). **The analyst spotted this during the project and corrected it.** The recorded result came from the corrected pipeline. The annotation is preserved in the analyst's fork (`osf.io/6zqct`, folder `moreh_rep_vAPR2025/`): the script carries the note "APRIL 2025 – Mistake in dataset here; the dataset associated with 'eb724' should be 'ZA4994_v3-0-0.dta'". The official Multi100 component (`osf.io/8rtwe`) preserved only the earlier, uncorrected folder; the fork publishes the full trail.

Coding can go wrong - even during a reanalysis! Keeping materials open and well documented helps spot any mistakes and correct them. That's the purpose of reproducibility.
:::

```{r}
#| label: fit-corrected
# The corrected panel: same construction, the right 2009 wave.
ten <- read_csv("data/teney_panel.csv") |>
  mutate(unemp_c = unemp - mean(unemp, na.rm = TRUE))

m_corrected <- feols(mcosmo ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = ten)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-corrected
#| tbl-cap: "The same constrained model on the corrected teney_panel.csv."
modelsummary(
  list("corrected (fixest)" = m_corrected),
  estimate  = "{estimate}",
  statistic = "t = {statistic}",
  gof_map   = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
  coef_rename = c("unemp_c" = "Unemployment (centred)")
)
```

On the corrected panel the coefficient is **−0.00347** with **t = −3.804** – exactly the value recorded for analyst C6HJR in the Multi100 dataset. The constrained result finally reproduces. Now make it your own.

Commit: `git commit -am "Task D1: reproduce the constrained result (t = -3.80)"`.
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-note title="Task D2 – Your analytical path (preregister, then run)"}
Pick **one** deviation from the specification menu on the workshop site ([the spec menu](https://codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/exercise/spec-menu.html)) – a different outcome scale, a different unemployment transform, adding GDP growth, a different estimator, a different sample window, or weights. Every option is *justifiable*; the discipline is choosing **with a reason**.

**TODO – write your choice and a two-sentence justification:**

> **Our spec:** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
>
> **Why (2 sentences):** \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Preregister it – this is the point of the task.** Commit your choice *before* you run it, with a message in this exact shape:

``` sh
git commit -am "prereg: <DV> ~ <IV> | <estimator> | <sample>"
```

*Only then* fill in the parameters below and run. The skeleton is a worked `fixest` example you adapt – change the variables, not the plumbing.

```{r}
#| label: my-spec
# --- your choices (edit these) --------------------------------------------
my_outcome  <- "mcosmo"     # e.g. "mcosmo" (constrained) or "mpos" (all positive)
my_exposure <- "unemp_c"    # e.g. "unemp_c", "unemp", "log(unemp)"
my_controls <- character(0) # e.g. "growth", or character(0) for none
my_fe       <- c("cntry", "year")  # e.g. c("cntry","year"), or just "cntry"

# --- build the formula and fit (you can leave this plumbing as is) ---------
rhs <- paste(c(my_exposure, my_controls), collapse = " + ")
fml <- as.formula(
  paste(my_outcome, "~", rhs, "|", paste(my_fe, collapse = " + "))
)

my_model <- feols(fml, data = ten)
my_model
```

```{r}
#| label: report
# Build your one-click submission link to the class-results form.
source("R/report_result.R")
report_result(
  my_model,
  spec = "mcosmo ~ unemp_c | twoway-FE | all 2004–2013"  # describe YOUR spec
)
```

Run this, then click the submission link it prints – your result lands as a dot on the live [Multiverse](https://codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/results.html) chart. (Do it now, or in Task E.)
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-note title="Task E – Publish and share"}
Get your report online and your result onto the live chart.

1.  **Render** one last time: `quarto render` (or just save while `quarto preview` is running).
2.  **Commit** your work: in Positron's **Source Control** panel, stage the changes, write a message (e.g. `Task E: final render and result`), and commit.
3.  **Push**: click **⋯ → Push** (or the sync arrows) in Source Control.
4.  **Submit your result** (if you have not already): run the `report_result()` chunk above and click the link it prints – your dot appears on the live [Multiverse](https://codemoreh.github.io/applied-replication/results.html) chart.
5.  **Share your repo:** post your repository URL to the [Discussion gallery](https://github.com/CodeMoreh/applied-replication/discussions).
6.  If you enabled Pages (Settings → Pages → Source: "GitHub Actions"), your live report appears at `https://<your-username>.github.io/replication-lab/` within a minute or two of the push.

Tick the passport boxes in `README.md` as you complete them – with a commit, of course.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-note title="Task F – Stretch (optional)"}
Time to spare? Pick one.

The Teney (2016) paper went through all three Rs in the SCORE programme – and got three different verdicts. The same paper's push-button reproduction **failed** (the author-provided Stata code referenced file names that did not match what was available for download), and its preregistered secondary-data replication returned a **sign flip** (a positive coefficient, b = 0.002, t = 2.004, in the opposite direction to the original). You can explore the full SCORE dossier – reproduction, robustness, and replication side by side – at <https://osf.io/h7432/> (SCORE parent node for this paper).

- **A second spec.** Change `my_outcome`, `my_exposure`, or `my_fe` above, rerun, and `report_result()` it. Two points on the class multiverse instead of one.

- **Fixed vs random effects.** Fit the random-effects version with `plm` and compare with a Hausman test – if it rejects, the fixed-effects estimate is the safer one.

  ```{r}
  #| label: hausman
  #| eval: false
  # Set eval: true to run. Compares within (FE) vs random effects.
  re <- plm(mcosmo ~ unemp_c, data = ten, index = c("cntry", "year"),
            effect = "twoways", model = "random")
  fe <- plm(mcosmo ~ unemp_c, data = ten, index = c("cntry", "year"),
            effect = "twoways", model = "within")
  phtest(fe, re)
  ```

- **Weight by sample size.** Country-years rest on very different respondent counts (`n_cy`); weight the model by `n_cy` and see whether the estimate moves.

  ```{r}
  #| label: weighted
  #| eval: false
  feols(mcosmo ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = ten, weights = ~ n_cy)
  ```

- **The mirror-image expectation.** The claim is about *positive* framing. Look at a **negative** dimension instead (`mneg`, or communitarian `mcomm`): if poor economic performance lowers positive framing, does it *raise* negative framing? Check the sign.

  ```{r}
  #| label: mirror
  #| eval: false
  feols(mneg ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = ten)
  ```

- **New data + new analysis?** You could even attempt to import some new country-level variables to test alongside the ones available in the dataset – for example, importing data from the World Bank, for which there is even a dedicated `R` package (see [https://vincentarelbundock.github.io/WDI/](https://vincentarelbundock.github.io/WDI/)) –, or download data for more recent years from the Eurobarometer to test for *generalisability*.

:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## References

Teney, C. (2016). Does the EU economic crisis undermine subjective Europeanization? Assessing the dynamics of citizens' EU framing between 2004 and 2013. *European Sociological Review*, 32(5), 619–633.

Lundberg, I., Johnson, R., & Stewart, B. M. (2021). What is your estimand? Defining the target quantity connects statistical evidence to theory. *American Sociological Review*, 86(3), 532–565.

Aczel, B., et al. (2026). The robustness of social science findings to analytical variability (Multi100). *Nature*, 652, 135–142.
